new_findings.title: 90–120 min onboarding video + live screen-share shadowing before solo shift

variants.claim: New chatters should receive a recorded onboarding video covering all platform basics, then spend 2–3 days shadow-watching live chats with commentary, before taking their own shift.
variants.what_they_do: Agency records a 90–120 minute onboarding video covering the OF platform, tools (e.g. Keta Hero), expectations, and dos/don'ts. New chatter then watches a current chatter screen-share with live commentary for 2–3 days. Only after that do they take their own shift. This replaces 2-hour per-chatter personal onboarding sessions that don't scale.

sources.quote: you're going to send the new chatter like a 90 to 120 minute video explaining all the basics
you let them watch you or your other chatters, screen share, let them check, give a live commentary

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chatting team (§8); no internal onboarding SOP exists. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, produce a single reusable onboarding video (90–120 min) covering Palm's OF accounts, tools, voice/persona guidelines, and rules. Pair it with a 2–3 day shadowing period with live commentary from a senior chatter or supervisor before the new hire goes solo. Eliminates redundant 1:1 onboarding time at scale.

new_findings.title: Daily chat review creates supervision illusion that prevents chatter coasting

variants.claim: Daily feedback—not weekly—is necessary because it both accelerates skill improvement 7x and creates a psychological sense of being watched that raises chatter output quality.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or supervisor reviews chatter conversations every single day and sends specific, actionable feedback on what was done wrong and how to fix it. The stated goal is twofold: (1) compound skill improvement through daily micro-corrections, and (2) ensure chatters know they will be caught quickly if they cut corners, preventing slack behavior.

sources.quote: the person who gives them daily feedback makes seven times the progress than you
If you think you're being supervised, you right now, imagine there's a camera in your room… you will just be like vlogging

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) with no internal feedback cadence. Build-toward: when in-house team launches, a daily review loop is foundational to quality control.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a daily async review ritual into the in-house chat SOP: each shift's chats are sampled (e.g. 5–10 conversations) by a supervisor, who sends written feedback via Telegram or a shared doc before the next shift starts. Even a lightweight daily touch beats weekly reviews on both performance and accountability.

new_findings.title: Weekly 20–30 min Socratic coaching call with weekly focus goal replaces directive feedback

variants.claim: Simply telling chatters what to do doesn't work; a weekly structured conversation that leads them to discover the reasoning themselves, then sets one concrete weekly focus goal, produces lasting behavior change.
variants.what_they_do: Every Monday, supervisor holds a 20–30 minute call with each chatter. Format: pull up specific chat examples, ask Socratic questions ('Why did you do it that way? What's the main reason we seduce?') so the chatter articulates the correct reasoning themselves. End of call: set ONE focus goal for the next 7 days (e.g. 'be more seductive'). Chatter sends written notes from the call. Next Monday opens by reviewing whether last week's goal was met before setting the new one.

sources.quote: You sit down on Monday with each chatter you have like a 20 to 30 minute time slot
for this week, let's set a focus. We want to set the focus for the next seven days till our next call

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatting coaching infrastructure exists at Palm (§8). This is the highest-fidelity build-toward tactic in the video — directly applicable to designing Palm's future in-house chat team management layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this as a standing Airtable or Notion record per chatter: columns for weekly call date, focus goal set, goal met (Y/N), and notes. Supervisors run the Monday cadence. Script the Socratic question bank for common scenarios (seduction, PPV push, objection handling) so any supervisor can run the call consistently without relying on improvisation.

new_findings.title: Socratic leading vs. directive telling: the skill gap separating elite from amateur agencies

variants.claim: The ability to make a chatter genuinely understand and want to apply a technique—rather than just comply in the moment and revert when unsupervised—is the core differentiator between high-revenue and failing agencies.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly trains managers/supervisors in coaching methodology: never just state 'do this differently,' but walk the chatter through the reasoning chain until they internalize it. If they only hear an instruction without understanding, they revert to old behavior the moment supervision lapses. The coaching skill (leading someone to learn vs. telling them what to do) is framed as a distinct, trainable competency for agency managers.

sources.quote: The art of explaining something to someone, making them understand and making them want to do it, is the actual skill that most agencies are lacking
what separates the high-level agencies with high level chatting teams to the amateurs to the side hustlers

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chatting management layer (§8). This finding applies at the manager/supervisor hiring level: when Palm builds its in-house chat team, supervisor selection criteria should include coaching ability, not just chatting ability.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When hiring Palm's first in-house chat supervisor, evaluate candidates on coaching ability explicitly (e.g. ask them to walk through a mock chat correction session). Create a supervisor training module covering Socratic questioning technique. Don't promote the best chatter automatically — best chatter ≠ best coach.

new_findings.title: Three-strike firing system + public praise for good work creates growth culture

variants.claim: A documented strike system for rule violations combined with public monetary rewards for strong performance creates an accountability culture that prevents low performers from degrading the whole team.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a formal strike system: if a chatter fails to follow explicit instructions (e.g. is late, unprofessional, ignores a directive), they receive a documented strike. Three strikes in a month = termination or 50% salary cut. Simultaneously, good work is publicly praised in the team channel and rewarded with cash bonuses (e.g. $50 spot bonus). Low performers are fired quickly because their presence is proven to drag down high performers, not the reverse.

sources.quote: a cheddar doesn't follow your instructions… give him a strike. If he has three strikes in a month, fire him or cut his salary in half
if people go the extra mile… publicly praise them, send them extra money, say 'Hey, I noticed you did good work. Here's 50 bucks'

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented performance management system for any employee type (§10 — THIN org structure, no scorecards or comp bands). This applies immediately to editors and Amin (contractor), not just future chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a lightweight strike/recognition system across all Palm contractors now (editors, Amin, future chatters): define 3–5 specific fireable behaviors (missed deadlines, repeated revision failures, no-shows), document strikes in Airtable or Slack, and tie them to a clear consequence schedule. Pair with a small discretionary bonus pool ($50–100/month) for publicly rewarding standout work. Low overhead, high accountability signal.

new_findings.title: Low performers contaminate high performers — remove them fast before culture erodes

variants.claim: Keeping low-performing chatters in a team drags high performers down to their level rather than lifting low performers up, making fast removal essential to maintaining team standards.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner applies a hard rule: when a low performer is identified, they are terminated quickly rather than rehabilitated indefinitely. The rationale is empirically observed: mixed teams of high and low performers trend toward low performance over time. Because signing new chatters is documented as a repeatable process (separate video), the cost of removing and replacing is low enough to justify zero tolerance for sustained underperformance.

sources.quote: If you put five high performers… in a room with five low performers… at the end of the week, you got two high performers left and the rest are also lazy
Get rid of the B and C flares

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a small contractor base (editors, Amin) with no documented termination or performance-exit criteria (§10). No systematic chatter team yet. This is both an immediate ops principle and a build-toward for the chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a probationary review at 30 days for any new Palm contractor. Define minimum acceptable output metrics per role (e.g. editor: X revisions per day with <Y error rate). If not met after one documented correction cycle, exit quickly. Codify this in a one-page hiring/offboarding SOP so it's not emotionally driven.

new_findings.topic: Agency SOP documentation & shared source of truth
new_findings.title: Build a Notion-based SOP library covering every agency function

variants.claim: Agencies that don't document their processes are side hustles, not businesses; a shared source of truth in Notion is what separates the two.
variants.what_they_do: Jason's agency maintains a Notion workspace with discrete SOP sections for every function: IG device setup, account setup, growth hacking, warmup, daily posting cadence, reel sourcing, profile optimization, paid promo, SEO method, content recording quality, boosting, metrics/split tests, Twitter setup, and AI face-swap methods. Each section contains only validated, proven procedures — no speculative content. New team members or models are onboarded directly from this shared doc.

sources.quote: we have fixed strategies and SOPs that work for us in different areas
outline what it is you're doing why you're doing it that way and strict SOPs so you can bring on somebody on board very easily

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has strong internal documentation for the content pipeline (§1), AI warmup (§4), and invoicing (§7), and uses Airtable as the operational spine, but there is no single consolidated SOP library equivalent to this Notion structure. Real-creator warmup/cadence (§4 GAP), traffic strategy (§5 GAP), and pricing rationale (§7 GAP) are explicitly un-documented. The principle matches what Palm aspires to, but the breadth of coverage is narrower.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm SOP Notion (or Confluence) workspace that mirrors this structure: one section per department, written for a new hire who knows nothing. Priority sections to fill first: real-creator IG warmup/cadence, reel sourcing method, paid promo contacts/process, and posting quality standards. Link to existing Airtable/codebase docs where they already exist to avoid duplication.

new_findings.title: Give every new creator a Notion dashboard at onboarding as a shared management system

variants.claim: Providing the model a structured dashboard at onboarding elevates professionalism for both parties and keeps everyone organized.
variants.what_they_do: When a new model is signed, she receives a pre-built Notion dashboard template along with a tutorial walkthrough. The dashboard serves as the management interface between the agency and the model — task tracking, content delivery, and communication all flow through it. The agency describes it as a change that 'changed a lot' for their operation.

sources.quote: this is the template for our dashboard that we give to a model it's essentially the management system that changed a lot for our agency
the dashboard helps them stay organized it helps us stay organized and it helps everyone just be a little bit more professional

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) that captures basic info and generates a contract, but there is no ongoing shared management dashboard given to the creator post-signing. Coordination today goes through Telegram and Airtable (internal only). The model has no visibility into her own pipeline status.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a creator-facing dashboard view (could be a read-only Airtable interface, a Notion page, or a section of Palm's own admin portal with a 'creator' Clerk role) that shows the model her content pipeline status, upcoming posting schedule, and weekly metrics. This increases creator buy-in and reduces back-and-forth. Pair with a short onboarding walkthrough video.

new_findings.title: Structure IG operations as a 9-section SOP covering every stage from setup to metrics

variants.claim: A complete, section-by-section IG playbook (device → setup → growth → warmup → daily usage → reel sourcing → profile optimization → paid promo → SEO → boosting → metrics) is necessary for consistent, scalable IG performance.
variants.what_they_do: Their Notion IG section has discrete chapters: (1) device setup, (2) new vs. aged account setup, (3) growth hacking / USA audience targeting, (4) warmup SOP, (5) daily usage & posting cadence (stories/posts/actions per day), (6) reel sourcing method (scraper vs. manual, delivery to model, selection criteria), (7) profile optimization for conversion, (8) paid promo (vendors, groups, contact list), (9) SEO method for daily growth, plus boosting SOPs and a 500-creator niche reference table.

sources.quote: we have an SOP for device setup, account setup, growth hacking and how to obtain USA audience, a warm-up SOP, daily usage and posting
how are we choosing those reels for the model all of these things that are very important for anybody that is on your team to know

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup SOP (§4) and a strong reel-sourcing pipeline via Apify scraping (§1). However, the real-creator IG side lacks a documented daily cadence (§1 GAP, §4 GAP), a paid promo SOP, and a profile optimization checklist. The 500-creator niche reference table is a useful analog to Palm's inspo sources list but organized differently (niche-filterable Excel rather than Airtable).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Port Palm's existing AI warmup playbook into a parallel real-creator IG SOP. Fill the five missing chapters first: (a) daily posting cadence targets, (b) paid promo vendor list and process, (c) profile optimization checklist for conversion, (d) USA audience targeting method, (e) reel sourcing delivery workflow (already exists in pipeline but should be written as a standalone SOP for non-technical team members). The niche-filterable creator reference table is worth building inside Airtable as an extension of `Inspo Sources`.

new_findings.title: SOP requiring creators to record outside native IG app to preserve 1080p quality

variants.claim: Recording inside Instagram and then downloading yields only 720p; a strict SOP for recording natively at 1080p and delivering files directly prevents fuzzy content.
variants.what_they_do: They have a documented SOP specifying that creators must not record inside the Instagram app when quality matters (especially lip-sync or audio-specific reels). The SOP covers how to record at 1080p via the native iPhone camera, how to deliver the file without compression, and when this matters vs. when a simple one-take with overlaid music is fine. This is especially called out for reels requiring specific edits or lip syncing.

sources.quote: when you're recording inside Instagram and you're downloading the content you're only receiving 720p and if they're recording inside Instagram sending you this 720p video that's why your content looks fuzzy
you need a strict SOP for exactly how to generate that content

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) receives content from creators and editors work on it, but there is no documented recording quality SOP specifying what format/resolution creators must deliver. If creators are filming inside IG and sending 720p files, editor output quality is inherently capped. This is a real gap in the real-creator content SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a one-page 'How to film your reel' SOP to the creator onboarding dashboard. Specify: film in iPhone Camera app (not IG), export as original quality, deliver via Google Drive (not compressed DM). Flag lip-sync/audio-match reels as requiring this method. Include a short Loom demo. This can be enforced by the editor — if a file arrives at 720p, it goes back to the creator rather than being edited at degraded quality.

new_findings.topic: IG Reel boosting — metrics-driven SOP with split tests
new_findings.title: Track cost-per-visit and run split tests on boosted Reels with a dedicated boosting SOP

variants.claim: Boosting IG Reels should be governed by a documented SOP covering cost-per-visit benchmarks, split test methodology, general guidelines, and a special protocol for new model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: They have a multi-part boosting SOP: (1) understanding cost-per-visit as the core metric, (2) how they've historically worked with it, (3) a split-test framework for testing boost variations, (4) recommended boosting guidelines as general rules, and (5) a separate SOP specifically for boosting new model accounts (presumably more aggressive early spend). All boosting decisions are metrics-driven and documented.

sources.quote: understanding cost per visit what does it mean how did we work with it then we have a section for metrics and split tests
we also have an SOP for boosting new models specifically

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm does not have a documented boosting strategy or paid promo SOP (§5 GAP, §1 GAP on posting cadence). There is no internal baseline on cost-per-visit targets or split test frameworks for IG paid promotion. This is a significant gap if any paid amplification is being done on creator accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Palm boosting SOP starting with: (a) define cost-per-visit as the primary boosting KPI and set a target range based on niche, (b) establish a split-test template (one variable at a time: audience vs. creative vs. placement), (c) write separate guidelines for new model accounts (higher spend tolerance in first 30 days to accelerate growth), (d) track all boost spend and results in Airtable. This can be a small Airtable table linked to creator records.

new_findings.topic: Twitter/X as secondary traffic channel with structured daily SOPs
new_findings.title: Run Twitter/X as a structured secondary traffic channel with daily growth SOPs, retweet groups, and comment-baiting

variants.claim: Twitter/X is a systematic traffic channel requiring its own SOP stack: account types, profile config, daily organic growth actions, retweet group participation, comment-baiting, shadow-ban avoidance, and content type selection by model type.
variants.what_they_do: Their Twitter blueprint has seven sections: (1) account types and setup rationale, (2) profile configuration for aesthetic, (3) daily organic growth SOPs (specific daily actions), (4) retweet group SOPs (correct vs. incorrect participation), (5) comment-baiting types and general SOP, (6) content type selection by model/account category, (7) shadow-ban avoidance. They also reference paid promo and outsourcing/hiring as Twitter-specific options. An 'adult network method' and 'free tube money method' are mentioned but not elaborated.

sources.quote: the Twitter blueprint — account types and setup, Twitter profile configuration, daily organic growth, retweet groups, comment baiting and the different types of comment baiting
a section for avoiding shadowbans and then lastly a section for outsourcing and hiring

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic strategy is entirely undocumented (§5 GAP). Twitter/X is not referenced anywhere in Palm's internal baseline. If any creators are posting on X, it is ad hoc. Retweet groups and comment-baiting are specific high-leverage tactics Palm has no equivalent for.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Assess whether any current Palm real creators have Twitter/X presence. If yes, build a minimal Twitter SOP covering daily actions, retweet group sourcing, and comment-baiting as a starting point. If no, add Twitter/X as a Phase 2 traffic channel in the SMM hub redesign (§11 in-flight) so the infrastructure is ready. The 'content type by model category' distinction is worth capturing — some creators/niches perform differently on X than IG and the strategy should differ.

new_findings.topic: AI face-swap as identity protection / beauty enhancement tool
new_findings.title: Maintain three distinct AI face-swap SOPs: ripped, beauty enhancement, and identity protection

variants.claim: AI face-swap is used operationally with three distinct methods for different purposes, each requiring its own documented SOP to avoid bad swaps that are worse than no swap.
variants.what_they_do: They have three named face-swap SOPs: (1) the 'ripped method' (presumably swapping from existing public content), (2) the 'beauty enhancement method' (improving the existing creator's appearance), and (3) the 'identity protection method' (masking the creator's real face). Each SOP covers when to use that method, how to execute it correctly, and quality guidelines — since a bad swap is called out as pointless or counterproductive.

sources.quote: three SOPs or three methods for AI face swap — the ripped method, the beauty enhancement method, and then the identity protection method
perfect guidelines for how to actually generate good swaps because a bad swap is pointless

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already produces AI social content for some real creators and has an AI persona program (§0/§3). The three-method framework (ripped / beauty enhancement / identity protection) is more granular than any documented Palm approach. The identity protection use case is directly relevant for real creators who want privacy; the beauty enhancement method is relevant for creators whose social content quality needs uplift.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the three-method framing in Palm's AI content SOP. Specifically: (a) document when identity protection face-swap is offered to real creators who want anonymity (this is a signing incentive too — §6 GAP), (b) establish quality guidelines so editors can reject bad swaps before they post. The 'ripped method' may raise IP/consent concerns — evaluate carefully before adding to Palm's SOP. Tag this finding for both the real-creator AI content workflow and the future AI persona line.

new_findings.topic: Horizontal scaling as the primary growth lever
new_findings.title: Frame agency growth as horizontal scaling of a proven, documented system — not optimization of one account

variants.claim: Once SOPs are documented and proven, growth comes from applying them horizontally across more models, not from doing more per model.
variants.what_they_do: The agency explicitly frames its documented SOP library as the enabler of horizontal scaling: once every section is filled in with proven methods, adding a new model is simply running the same playbook. They contrast this with the 'side hustle' operator who has knowledge in their head but can't replicate it.

sources.quote: when it comes to these strategies it's just a matter of horizontal scaling at the end of the day
put it on paper and you're going to see what's missing — right now it's in here and you're like I know exactly what to do but put it on paper and you're going to see what's missing

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a horizontal scaling lever most agencies lack — the AI persona program (§0/§3). For real creators, Palm's content pipeline (§1) is systematized and repeatable, but gaps in real-creator warmup (§4), traffic (§5), and pricing (§7) mean the playbook isn't yet fully transferable to a new team member or a new creator without founder involvement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use horizontal scaling as the explicit design goal when filling in Palm's SOP gaps. For each gap section (real-creator warmup, traffic, boosting, paid promo, pricing), ask: 'Could a new hire execute this without asking Josh or Evan?' If not, it's not SOP-ready. Tie SOP completion milestones to the hiring plan (§10 GAP) — you can only safely hire when the playbook exists for that role.

new_findings.title: Track acquisition funnel metrics to distinguish a volume problem from a quality problem

variants.claim: Most agencies misdiagnose traffic or monetization as their constraint when the real issue is signing low-quality creators who can never succeed regardless of what traffic system is applied.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan instructs owners to run weekly acquisition numbers: conversations initiated → qualified replies → calls booked → show-up rate → signed → actually commercially viable. 'Commercially viable' is defined not by looks or follower count but by ability to produce content consistently, take direction, fit the agency's avatar, and communicate. He says 99% of creators will never make it statistically and no traffic system fixes a bad creator.

sources.quote: commercially viable, I don't mean they're good-looking... I mean a creator who can produce the content, take the direction that fits your avatar, that communicates, that stays consistent, that actually has the potential to monetize
statistically, 99% of creators are never going to make it regardless what magical traffic system you put them into

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented funnel tracking or definition of 'commercially viable creator' (§6 THIN). The Creators table captures onboarding status but not pre-signing pipeline metrics. Palm has no written ICA (ideal creator avatar) criteria to qualify against.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's ICA in writing: content consistency, communication quality, niche fit, audience baseline. Add a pre-signing pipeline tracker (conversations → qualified → called → signed → launched → revenue-generating) to Airtable. Review weekly. This will surface whether Palm's constraint is volume of outreach or quality of who gets signed.

new_findings.title: Measure days-to-launch as a KPI and compress it to one day or less

variants.claim: Every day between signing and a creator going live is lost revenue, and slow activation is often misread as a traffic or monetization problem.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan frames creator activation as its own constraint bucket. He audits: days from signing to full launch, whether one person owns the onboarding, whether the creator knows exactly what content is needed and by when, whether expectations and consequences were set, and whether the chat team is loaded with content before traffic even starts. His benchmark improvement example: cutting 7 days to 1 day = 6 extra days of revenue per creator, which compounds at scale (3–5 creators/day onboarded).

sources.quote: how many days pass between us signing a creator and then actually getting everything set up and getting it launched
if that's your constraint, it takes 7 days to onboard a creator. If you managed to get that seven days down to one day, that's an extra six days of potential revenue

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an onboarding form wizard that captures creator data (§6) but no documented SLA for time-to-launch, no single named owner for activation, and no checklist confirming the chat team is briefed before traffic begins. The SMM hub redesign (§11) is improving account setup but activation speed is not a tracked KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 'launched' state clearly (accounts live, content plan briefed, chat team loaded, first post scheduled). Track days-to-launched per creator in Airtable. Assign one person (likely an ops/onboarding role) as the sole owner. Set an internal SLA of 48 hours or less. Surface this metric in the monthly constraint audit.

new_findings.title: Break traffic into a 5-step conversion funnel with per-step metrics rather than diagnosing 'traffic is bad'

variants.claim: Vague 'traffic is bad' diagnoses are useless; agencies must measure views → profile visits → link clicks → subscribers → average spend to find exactly where the funnel breaks.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan requires organic traffic to be tracked as: pieces of content out → views pulled → % visiting profile → % clicking link → % subscribing → average spend per fan. He also distinguishes top-of-funnel content (designed to go viral) from bottom-of-funnel content (designed to convert existing viewers to subscribers). A common misdiagnosis he sees: great views but poor conversions, caused by all content being top-of-funnel with no conversion-oriented posts. For paid traffic (cloak strategy) he tracks CPM, CTR, CPC, landing page CVR, cost per subscriber, and revenue per subscriber.

sources.quote: how many pieces of content actually go out, how many views are they pulling in, what percentage visit the profile, what percentage click, what percentage subscribe
we're missing that bottom of funnel content and all the content we make is just top of funnel content. It's just literally designed to go viral and that's it, it's not designed to convert

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is strong on inspo-driven production and scheduling but does not document a top-of-funnel vs. bottom-of-funnel content split strategy. Posting cadence is not codified (§1 GAP). There is no documented traffic conversion funnel with per-step metrics. Publer Phase 3 (§5) adds monitoring/alerts but no funnel tracking.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a per-creator traffic funnel tracker: views, profile visits, link clicks, new subs, avg spend. Review weekly. Audit each creator's content mix: what % is top-of-funnel (viral) vs. bottom-of-funnel (conversion)? For creators with high views but low sub conversion, prescribe a minimum of X bottom-of-funnel posts per week. Add this as a field in the Posts or Assets table.

new_findings.title: Track 8 monetization KPIs to find exactly where revenue stops flowing on the OF side

variants.claim: An agency can double its revenue without adding any traffic by fixing a monetization constraint, but only if it tracks the right per-chatter and per-creator metrics.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's monetization audit list: revenue per subscriber, average spend per fan, new subscriber conversion rate, average first purchase size, time-to-first-purchase, PPV purchase rate, revenue per chatter-hour, rebuild rate, reactivation revenue, revenue from new vs. existing fans, performance broken down by chatter × shift × creator. He argues one agency can make 2× another off identical subscriber counts purely through better monetization, and that blaming chatters is often wrong — the pricing model or strategy set by management is frequently the actual issue.

sources.quote: what's the revenue per subscriber, what's the average spend per fan, what's the new subscriber conversion rate, what's the average first purchase... what's the PPV purchase rate, what's the revenue per chatter hour, what's the rebuild rate, what's the reactivation revenue
you can literally be one agency and make twice as much as another agency off the exact same number of subscribers and the same number of traffic

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting today (§8) and has no internal SOP, scripts, or monetization KPIs. The Fan Tracker / Whale Hunting feature (§9) collects some fan-level data but per-chatter and per-shift performance tracking is not documented. This is a direct build-toward for Palm's in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), instrument these 8 KPIs from day one: revenue/subscriber, avg spend/fan, first-purchase size and time, PPV purchase rate, revenue/chatter-hour, rebuild rate, reactivation revenue, new vs. returning fan revenue split. Build a chatter performance dashboard in Airtable broken down by chatter × shift × creator. Use this to isolate whether low revenue is a chatter skill issue or a strategy/pricing issue before making personnel decisions.

new_findings.title: Flag concentration risk when one creator exceeds 50% of agency revenue and treat it as a primary constraint

variants.claim: An agency whose top creator produces 50%+ of revenue is one lost creator away from catastrophic collapse, and this risk must be treated as the primary constraint above all growth work.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan defines concentration risk explicitly: if one creator = 50%+ of revenue, that creator's departure can wipe the business. He cites a real case: agency at $300K/month lost its top creator, who took her referral network with her, and the agency went to zero. His retention audit questions: How often are creators updated? Do they understand their own numbers? Do they know what the agency does with their 50%? Are wins communicated? Is content request timing consistent? Does the creator feel like a genuine partner, not just a content supplier?

sources.quote: what percentage of your revenue comes from your largest creator. If one of your creators produces 50% or more of your entire agency's revenue, you've now got concentration risk
their main creator got really pissed off... she left, she took all of her friends that she referred with her and they literally went to zero and they were making 300 grand a month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no written creator retention strategy or churn-signal SOP (§9 GAP). The Fan Tracker exists for fan retention but not creator retention. There is no documented process for communicating agency value back to creators or tracking creator satisfaction. Palm's commission is set at onboarding (§7) but the rationale is not explained to creators in a structured way.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'revenue concentration' metric to Palm's monthly constraint audit: what % of total agency revenue comes from the top creator? If >40%, treat this as a primary constraint requiring immediate acquisition and diversification effort. Build a simple creator success cadence: weekly or bi-weekly check-in, revenue report shared with creator showing their numbers and where the agency's cut goes, explicit win-sharing. Document this as a retention SOP.

new_findings.title: Match the constraint you work on to your current revenue stage — don't solve future-stage problems

variants.claim: Each revenue band has a predictable primary constraint, and solving the wrong stage's problem wastes months while the real bottleneck sits untouched.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan maps constraints to stages: <$10K = foundational (make one creator work, 200 outreaches/day, basic traffic); $10K–$30K = validation (understand why it worked, can you repeat it); $30K–$100K = replication (reproduce across multiple creators, build consistency, reduce single-platform dependence); $100K–$300K = management (team runs without founder touching every department, managers hold standards, retain OG creators); $300K+ = scale/diversification (add creators without weakening delivery, cut dependence on any single employee/creator/account, allocate profit into proven channels). He explicitly warns against building complex systems before signing a first creator.

sources.quote: below 10 grand a month your problem is usually foundational... 10K to 30K the problem then usually becomes some sort of validation... 30k to 100k the problem then is usually replication... 100K and 300K problem is usually management... above 300k the problem always then becomes scale
do not try and solve a 300 grand a month agency problem while you're making 10 grand a month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented stage model or stage-gated priorities. Based on the codebase (§6 THIN, §7 THIN), Palm appears to be in the replication-to-management transition zone. The SMM hub redesign (§11) and invoicing automation (§7) suggest Palm is systematizing, which aligns with the $30K–$100K replication stage concerns. However, without a formal stage assessment, Palm risks building $300K-stage infrastructure while $30K-stage problems (consistent creator results, acquisition volume) may still be the real constraint.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have Josh and Evan explicitly agree on Palm's current revenue stage and the one constraint that matches it. Write it down. Use Dylan's stage map as a starting template. Review quarterly. Defer any initiative that belongs to a future stage — use this as a filter for the product roadmap (e.g., don't build complex org charts or multi-channel diversification tooling until the current stage's constraint is cleared).

new_findings.title: Use 'Emotion → Evidence → Constraint → Action → Reassess' loop to diagnose agency bottlenecks

variants.claim: Agencies stay stuck because owners react emotionally to symptoms rather than tracing them to a single root constraint, and a five-step diagnostic loop breaks that pattern.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan teaches a named framework: strip emotion, look at evidence, identify the one constraint, take action, reassess, then repeat. He argues every agency has only one primary constraint at a time across six possible buckets: creator acquisition, creator activation, traffic, monetization, retention/fulfillment, and team/management. The loop should run in days-to-weeks, not months.

sources.quote: emotion, evidence, constraint, action, reassessment. Something goes wrong, you strip out the emotion, you look at the evidence, you find the constraint, you take action, then you reassess

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented constraint-identification or agency-health diagnostic process (§7 GAP, §9 GAP). Josh-led strategy calls exist but there is no formalized periodic review loop or written decision framework. Without this, Palm risks the exact emotional/reactive decision-making Dylan describes.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this framework as Palm's internal operating cadence. Implement a monthly 'constraint audit' where Josh and Evan explicitly name the current #1 bottleneck, cite the data proving it, assign one owner, and set a 30-day resolution target. Build a lightweight dashboard that surfaces the six constraint buckets at a glance.
